pathophysiology_summary: Raynaud's phenomenon is episodic, reversible vasospasm of the digital arteries and arterioles, typically triggered by cold or emotional stress. It classically produces a sharply demarcated, sequential triphasic colour change: pallor (white) as the digital arteries constrict and flow ceases, cyanosis (blue) as deoxygenated blood stagnates, and rubor (red) with throbbing pain and paraesthesia as reactive hyperaemia restores flow. Primary Raynaud's (Raynaud's disease) occurs without an underlying disorder - it is bilateral, symmetric, involves the fingers (and sometimes toes), spares tissue (no ulceration, gangrene or digital pitting), and has normal peripheral pulses and a negative autoimmune screen; it is a benign, lifelong but manageable condition whose mainstay of treatment is thermal protection and avoidance/limitation of vasoconstrictor triggers. Secondary Raynaud's is associated with an underlying disorder (most often connective-tissue disease such as systemic sclerosis/scleroderma, SLE or Sjogren's) and is suggested by later onset, asymmetry, severe attacks, digital ulceration/pitting, abnormal nailfold capillaries or a positive ANA - hence the value of outpatient screening bloods. Several drugs precipitate or aggravate vasospasm, notably non-selective beta-blockers (through unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction), so a recently commenced beta-blocker for migraine prophylaxis is a reversible aggravating factor to review. The clinical importance in an acute presentation is that a sudden, painful, white-and-numb digit can be indistinguishable by phone from acute arterial embolism/critical digital ischaemia; the discriminating features favouring benign Raynaud's are bilateral symmetric involvement, the complete reversible triphasic sequence, normal/strong peripheral pulses, brisk capillary refill and the absence of necrosis or a single cold pulseless limb. Because pulse palpation, capillary refill and viability assessment cannot be performed over telehealth, the safe course is active warming to abort the attack plus urgent same-day in-person assessment to confirm perfusion and exclude a limb/digit-threatening cause, with a low threshold for emergency care if a digit remains white/numb despite warming or develops blackening/ulceration.

differential.evidence_basis: A cold-triggered, sharply demarcated triphasic (white-blue-red) colour change in the fingers of both hands with reactive hyperaemic pain is the classic picture of Raynaud's vasospasm.

differential.diagnosis: Acute arterial embolism / critical digital ischaemia
differential.evidence_basis: A sudden painful white numb digit can mimic embolic occlusion; must be excluded, though bilateral symmetric involvement and a fully reversible sequence argue strongly against a single-limb embolus.
Must be screened for over time (autoimmune features, ulceration, asymmetry); not excludable at this single acute contact.
Can cause numbness/paraesthesia, but does not produce the sharply demarcated, visible colour sequence tied to the sensory symptoms.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should recognise a benign Raynaud's vasospastic attack while still safety-netting and excluding critical digital ischaemia; the correct action is active warming plus urgent same-day in-person perfusion check, not immediate over-escalation to an ambulance nor remote dismissal.

differential.diagnosis: Primary Raynaud's phenomenon (acute vasospastic attack, aggravated by a recently started beta-blocker)
differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral symmetric fingers (DI-003), full reversible triphasic sequence (DI-001) with cold/stress trigger (DI-002), no necrosis/ulceration and returning colour and warmth (DI-005), plus a recently commenced beta-blocker (DI-006), all point to benign primary Raynaud's.

differential.diagnosis: Acute arterial embolism / critical digital ischaemia
differential.evidence_basis: Bilateral symmetry, reversibility and the absence of a single cold pulseless limb make embolism unlikely, but pulses/perfusion cannot be confirmed over telehealth so it must be safety-netted and confirmed in person.
Remains to be screened out with outpatient autoimmune bloods (e.g. ANA); no current features of established connective-tissue disease disclosed.
The visible, sharply demarcated colour changes matching the onset/offset of the sensory symptoms are not explained by neuropathy alone.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen this is benign primary Raynaud's aggravated by a beta-blocker: active warming advice, review/cessation of the beta-blocker with the GP, urgent same-day in-person confirmation of perfusion, outpatient screening for secondary causes, with acute-digital-ischaemia safety-netting.

differential.diagnosis: Primary Raynaud's phenomenon (acute vasospastic attack) - benign, with a beta-blocker as an aggravating factor and secondary causes to be screened
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis. Pulse palpation, capillary refill/viability and autoimmune bloods need in-person assessment; the correct action is active warming, an urgent same-day perfusion check, a medication review of the beta-blocker, and routine outpatient screening - with emergency care if a digit stays white/numb or blackens.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final telehealth framing is benign primary Raynaud's - do not over-triage a resolving, viable, bilateral vasospastic attack to an ambulance, but do arrange urgent same-day in-person confirmation of perfusion, review the beta-blocker, and safety-net for critical digital ischaemia.

red_flags.description: A digit that stays white/numb despite active warming, or develops blackening, ulceration or sores (critical/irreversible digital ischaemia) - ABSENT at present but must be safety-netted
red_flags.clinical_significance: Persistent pallor/numbness or tissue necrosis would indicate true critical digital ischaemia (rather than reversible vasospasm), a limb/digit-threatening emergency; their absence, with returning colour and warmth, supports urgent same-day rather than immediate escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If a digit progresses to fixed ischaemia/gangrene and is not acted on urgently, tissue/digit loss can result.

red_flags.description: Features of a single cold, pale, pulseless, painful, paralysed limb (acute arterial embolism) - would change this from bilateral vasospasm to a surgical emergency
red_flags.clinical_significance: Unilateral, non-reversing ischaemia with absent pulses would indicate embolic occlusion needing immediate emergency care; the bilateral symmetric reversible pattern here argues against it, but pulses cannot be palpated over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed embolic occlusion risks limb loss within hours.

red_flags.description: Normal, strong bilateral radial and ulnar pulses, brisk capillary refill (<2 s), and no tissue necrosis/ulceration on clinician examination
red_flags.clinical_significance: These clinician-elicited findings confirm intact large-vessel perfusion and viability, supporting benign reversible vasospasm over embolic occlusion; they cannot be obtained over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not applicable to telehealth - requires clinician examination; the correct action is urgent in-person confirmation of perfusion.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A dramatic, painful, colour-changing but bilateral, symmetric and already-reversing attack with intact viability is benign Raynaud's, not an ambulance-level embolic emergency; reflexive T5 escalation is over-triage.

The opposite error: reassuring purely by chat without arranging in-person confirmation of pulses/perfusion risks missing an embolic occlusion or critical digital ischaemia that cannot be palpated over telehealth.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Missing the beta-blocker as an aggravator
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A recently commenced beta-blocker is a common precipitant/aggravator of digital vasospasm; failing to flag it for GP review is a missed reversible factor.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failing to screen for secondary Raynaud's
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not arranging outpatient screening (e.g. ANA) for an underlying connective-tissue disease misses secondary Raynaud's over the longer term.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of radial and ulnar (and digital) pulses and their upstrokes
Capillary refill and limb/digit temperature and viability assessment
Examination for tissue necrosis, ulceration, digital pitting or nailfold capillary changes
Screening bloods for secondary causes (e.g. ANA/autoimmune screen)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Advise immediate active warming (place the hands under warm - not hot - running water or into the armpits at the first sign of pallor) to abort the attack, and arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment to palpate pulses, check capillary refill and confirm the fingers are perfused and viable and to exclude an embolic/critical ischaemic cause; review the recently commenced beta-blocker with the GP, and arrange routine outpatient screening for secondary causes - with IMMEDIATE emergency care if a finger stays white/numb despite warming or turns black or develops sores.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Either extreme is unsafe: over-escalating a resolving benign bilateral vasospasm to an emergency ambulance is over-triage, while dismissing it remotely without confirming perfusion risks missing embolic occlusion or critical digital ischaemia.
